beginner.question: What is the <footer> element?
beginner.answer: The <footer> element represents the footer of a page or section, typically containing copyright, contact info, and related links.

beginner.question: Can a page have more than one <footer>?
beginner.answer: Yes, a page can have multiple <footer> elements. One page-level footer and additional footers inside articles or sections.

beginner.question: What is the default display of <footer>?
beginner.answer: <footer> is a block-level element by default.

beginner.question: What kind of content belongs in a footer?
beginner.answer: Copyright notices, legal links, contact information, sitemaps, social media links, and back-to-top links.

beginner.question: Is <footer> inside <article> a landmark?
beginner.answer: No, only page-level <footer> has the contentinfo landmark role. Inside an article, it has no landmark role.

beginner.question: Can <footer> contain headings?
beginner.answer: Yes, <footer> can contain heading elements for organizing sections within the footer.

beginner.question: What element should I use for contact info in a footer?
beginner.answer: Use the <address> element for contact information in a footer.

beginner.question: Can <footer> contain <nav>?
beginner.answer: Yes, <footer> commonly contains <nav> for footer navigation links.

beginner.question: What is the difference between <footer> and <div>?
beginner.answer: <footer> has semantic meaning representing a footer region. <div> is a generic container with no semantic meaning.

beginner.question: What does the contentinfo ARIA role mean?
beginner.answer: contentinfo is a landmark role that indicates metadata about the page, such as copyright and legal links.

intermediate.question: What is the implicit ARIA role of a page-level <footer>?
intermediate.answer: A page-level <footer> has an implicit role of 'contentinfo'. When nested inside an article or section, it has no implicit role.

intermediate.question: How should you handle multiple <footer> elements for accessibility?
intermediate.answer: Only the page-level <footer> should have role='contentinfo' (implicit). Provide aria-label on footer <nav> elements to distinguish them from other nav landmarks.

intermediate.question: What elements cannot be nested inside a <footer>?
intermediate.answer: A <footer> must not contain <header>, <main>, or another <footer>.

intermediate.question: When should you use <small> inside a <footer>?
intermediate.answer: Use <small> for fine print, legal disclaimers, and copyright text that is less prominent.

intermediate.question: How does the <footer> element interact with the HTML5 outline?
intermediate.answer: <footer> does not create a new section in the document outline. It is a sectioning root but does not affect heading hierarchy.

intermediate.question: Can you use CSS Grid inside a <footer>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, CSS Grid or Flexbox are recommended for creating multi-column footer layouts.

intermediate.question: What is the correct way to add social media links in a footer?
intermediate.answer: Wrap social media links in a <nav> element with aria-label='Social media links' inside the <footer>.

intermediate.question: How should back-to-top links be implemented in a footer?
intermediate.answer: Use an anchor link: Back to top targeting an element with id='top' at the top of the page.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the <address> element?
intermediate.answer: The <address> element provides contact information for the nearest <article> or <body> ancestor.

intermediate.question: Can a <footer> contain a <form>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, <footer> can contain a <form> for newsletter signups or other tangential form interactions.

advanced.question: How does the <footer> contentinfo role affect screen reader navigation?
advanced.answer: Screen readers announce 'contentinfo landmark' for page-level footers, allowing users to quickly jump to footer content using landmark navigation.

advanced.question: What happens when you have multiple page-level <footer> elements without proper scoping?
advanced.answer: If multiple <footer> elements are direct children of <body>, all get the contentinfo role, which may confuse screen readers. Only one should be the page-level footer.

advanced.question: How does the <footer> element behave in print stylesheets?
advanced.answer: In print, the <footer> typically retains its content but may have additional print-specific information like page URLs or print dates.

advanced.question: What is the interaction between <footer> and CSS position: sticky?
advanced.answer: A sticky footer pattern uses CSS to keep the footer at the bottom: body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; min-height: 100vh; } footer { margin-top: auto; }.

advanced.question: How can you make a footer stick to the bottom of the viewport when content is short?
advanced.answer: Use flexbox: body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; min-height: 100vh; } main { flex: 1; } footer { flex-shrink: 0; }.

advanced.question: What are safe-area-inset-* CSS environment variables and how do they relate to footers?
advanced.answer: env(safe-area-inset-bottom) accounts for notched device bottom areas. Apply as padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom) on page footer.

advanced.question: How should you handle the footer in a mobile-first responsive design?
advanced.answer: On mobile, footer content is often stacked vertically. Use media queries to switch to multi-column layout on larger screens. Hide non-essential links.

advanced.question: What accessibility issues can arise from placing too many links in a footer?
advanced.answer: Excessive footer links create navigation burden for screen reader users. Group links with <nav aria-label='...'> and limit to essential links only.

advanced.question: How does the <footer> element work with Shadow DOM?
advanced.answer: In Shadow DOM, <footer> inside a shadow root creates its own contentinfo scope separate from the light DOM page footer.

advanced.question: What are the SEO implications of footer content?
advanced.answer: Footer links pass link equity but are weighted less than main content links. Important pages should be linked from main content rather than exclusively from the footer.
